architecture: Layered/Clean: Controllers (or Minimal API endpoints) -> Services -> Repositories -> EF Core DbContext.
Dependency injection via the built-in container; register services in Program.cs with correct lifetime.
Separate DTOs (request/response) from EF entities; map explicitly or via a mapper.
async all the way: I/O methods return Task/Task<T> and accept a CancellationToken.
conventions: Enable nullable reference types; treat nullable warnings as errors.
Use records for immutable DTOs; use ActionResult<T> / Results.* for responses.
Validate input (DataAnnotations or FluentValidation); never trust request bodies.
Do not block on async (no .Result/.Wait()); propagate CancellationToken.
One responsibility per class; keep controllers thin — logic lives in services.
lint: Run dotnet format; honor .editorconfig and enabled analyzers.
No compiler warnings in changed files; XML doc comments on public APIs.
Run dotnet test; keep unit tests for service logic.
templates: scaffold-data: Entity + DbSet + request/response DTOs + Repository interface/implementation + Service method; add EF migration if the schema changes.
scaffold-screen (endpoint): Controller action or Minimal API endpoint with route attributes, request/response DTO, validation, and typed result.
Follow the existing project structure; do not introduce a new pattern.
design-system: API contract: return RFC 7807 ProblemDetails for errors uniformly.
Follow REST conventions (verbs, status codes, plural resource routes).
Version the API; expose Swagger/OpenAPI as the contract source of truth.
Keep response DTOs stable; never leak EF entities directly.
library-docs: EF Core for data access; migrations via dotnet ef.
Common: FluentValidation, AutoMapper/Mapster, Serilog, MediatR — match what the repo uses.
Configuration via IOptions<T>; secrets via user-secrets/environment, never hardcoded.
